sound_type: cackling + soft clucks -- quiet alarm/territorial cackling and soft clucking contact calls; the quiet sounds of the alpine zone; E=3
ecosystem: Alpine tundra -- above treeline year-round. Alpine meadows, talus slopes, snowfields, krummholz edges. The most extreme habitat of any Phasianidae entry. The only bird in North America that lives above treeline year-round.
season: Year-round alpine resident. Breeding May-July. Never descends below treeline. Snow roosting in winter.
family: Phasianidae (6th and FINAL entry. Closes the family. 153-158 complete.)

coordinates.description: White-tailed Ptarmigan in alpine zones of the North Cascades, Mount Rainier, and other high peaks of Washington. Above treeline (6,000-7,000+ ft).

taxonomy_note.description: SIXTH and FINAL Phasianidae entry. Closes the family. Only year-round alpine bird in N.A. Pure white winter plumage. Smallest grouse (300-400g). Cackling + soft clucks. E=3 -- quietest Phasianidae. The extreme endpoint of the family arc.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Phasianidae: sixth and final entry. Wild Turkey (153) + Dusky Grouse (154) + Ruffed Grouse (155) + Sooty Grouse (156) + Spruce Grouse (157) + White-tailed Ptarmigan (158). Complete.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Lagopus from Greek 'lagos' (hare) + 'pous' (foot) -- hare-footed, referring to the feathered feet. leucura from Greek 'leukos' (white) + 'oura' (tail) -- white-tailed.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Lagopus -- first and only entry. The ptarmigan genus. Distinguished by feathered feet and seasonal plumage change.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Sixth and final Phasianidae. Alpine extreme. Quietest entry. Highest elevation. Closes the family arc.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Cackling: 1000-4000 Hz. Clucks: 500-2000 Hz. Higher-pitched than booming grouse species.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Cackling: 1-3 seconds. Clucks: individual 0.1s, series variable.
song_metrics.note_structure: CACKLING: Low rattling 'kuk-kuk-kuk-kuk'. Scratchy, dry. SOFT CLUCKS: Very quiet 'cluck-cluck' contact notes.
song_metrics.note_types: Cackling (alarm/territorial -- vocal), soft clucks (contact -- vocal), screaming (alarm when flushed -- vocal, rare).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Cackling: rapid series of 'kuk' notes. Clucking: irregular soft series for flock cohesion.
song_metrics.timbre: Cackling: scratchy, rattling, dry. Clucks: soft, intimate. Both quiet by grouse standards. Adapted for close-range communication in exposed alpine habitat.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Very low. Cackling audible 20-50 meters in calm conditions. Clucks audible 5-15 meters. Easily masked by alpine wind.
song_metrics.tempo: Cackling: rapid kuk notes. Clucking: moderate.
song_metrics.context: Breeding season (May-July). Alarm. Contact between flock members. Hen-chick communication.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Slight variation across range. Washington birds at southern extent of primary range.
song_metrics.key_property: CACKLING + SOFT CLUCKS -- quiet, intimate, alpine. E=3. SIXTH AND FINAL PHASIANIDAE. Only year-round alpine bird. Pure white winter plumage. Smallest grouse. The extreme endpoint.

plumage.overall: SUMMER: Mottled brown, gray, and white. Cryptic against alpine rocks and tundra. WINTER: Pure white. Entire bird white except bill and eyes. Complete seasonal transformation.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: White tail (diagnostic -- other ptarmigan species have black tails). Winter: pure white. Summer: mottled brown/gray with white wings. Feathered feet year-round.
plumage.seasonal_variation: The most dramatic seasonal plumage change of any PNW bird. Brown (summer) to pure white (winter). 6-8 weeks per transition. Photoperiod-driven.
plumage.flight_pattern: Burst flight when flushed. Short distance. Usually prefers to walk or freeze. Reluctant to fly in alpine wind.

physical_description.overall: Length: 30-33 cm. Weight: 300-400 g. Smallest North American grouse. Compact. Feathered feet (snowshoe adaptation).
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: White tail (all seasons). Feathered feet. In winter, pure white. In summer, mottled brown with white wings.
physical_description.field_id: Small grouse above treeline. In winter, pure white against snow -- virtually invisible. In summer, mottled brown among rocks. Listen for soft cackling in calm conditions. The tameness and alpine habitat are diagnostic.

nodes.title: Year-Round Alpine Residency
nodes.description: Only North American bird above treeline year-round. Never descends. Snow roosting. Willow bud diet. -40C endurance. Energy conservation. The extreme edge of avian habitat adaptation.

nodes.title: Seasonal Plumage Transformation
nodes.description: Complete brown-to-white plumage change. 6-8 weeks per transition. Photoperiod-driven. White tail diagnostic. Virtually invisible on snow. The most dramatic seasonal color change of any PNW bird.

nodes.title: Phasianidae Complete (153-158)
nodes.description: Six entries across six degrees. Ascending elevation (forest floor to alpine tundra). Descending energy (E=6 to E=3). Loudest (Wild Turkey) to quietest (White-tailed Ptarmigan). The family arc is complete.

nodes.title: Climate Change Vulnerability
nodes.description: Treeline advancing upslope with warming. Alpine zone shrinking. Habitat contraction. The ptarmigan's world is getting smaller. An indicator species for climate impact on alpine ecosystems.

cross_references.relationship: ELEVATION ASCENT! Spruce Grouse in boreal forest (157, E=4) to Ptarmigan above treeline (158, E=3). From forest to beyond forest. Energy descends.
FAMILY ARC! Turkey (153, E=6, low elevation) to Ptarmigan (158, E=3, alpine). Complete Phasianidae arc: 6 species, ascending elevation, descending energy.
MIGRATION CONTRAST! Dusky/Sooty reverse-migrate upslope in winter. Ptarmigan lives there year-round. No migration needed -- already at the top.
CAMOUFLAGE TYPES! Sooty Grouse: ventriloquial sound camouflage. Ptarmigan: pure white visual camouflage. Both masters of disappearance.
